title: Understanding the Role of Large Language Models in Software Engineering: Evidence from an Industry Survey
summary: The rapid advancement of Large Language Models (LLMs) is reshaping software engineering by profoundly influencing coding, documentation, and system maintenance practices. As these tools become deeply embedded in developers' daily workflows, understanding how they are used has become essential. This paper reports an empirical study of LLM adoption in software engineering, based on a survey of 46 industry professionals with diverse educational backgrounds and levels of experience. The results reveal positive perceptions of LLMs, particularly regarding faster resolution of technical questions, improved documentation support, and enhanced source code standardization. However, respondents also expressed concerns about cognitive dependence, security risks, and the potential erosion of technical autonomy. These findings underscore the need for critical and supervised use of LLM-based tools. By grounding the discussion in empirical evidence from industry practice, this study bridges the gap between academic discourse and real-world software development. The results provide actionable insights for developers and researchers seeking to adopt and evolve LLM-based technologies in a more effective, responsible, and secure manner, while also motivating future research on their cognitive, ethical, and organizational implications.

AI.tldr: A survey of 46 software engineers reveals both benefits and risks of LLM adoption, emphasizing the need for measured implementation to balance efficiency gains with security and autonomy.
AI.motivation: The rapid integration of LLMs into software engineering workflows necessitates understanding their adoption patterns, benefits, and risks to ensure responsible and effective usage.
AI.method: An empirical study based on a survey of 46 industry professionals with diverse educational backgrounds and experience levels.
AI.result: Respondents reported benefits such as faster technical question resolution, improved documentation, and code standardization, but raised concerns about cognitive dependence, security risks, and erosion of technical autonomy.
AI.conclusion: The study underscores the need for critical and supervised use of LLM-based tools in software engineering. It bridges academic and industry practices, providing actionable insights for developers and researchers while motivating future research on ethical, cognitive, and organizational implications of LLMs.

title: Fairness Is Not Just Ethical: Performance Trade-Off via Data Correlation Tuning to Mitigate Bias in ML Software
comment: Accepted to the 48th International Conference on Software Engineering (ICSE 2026) Research Track
summary: Traditional software fairness research typically emphasizes ethical and social imperatives, neglecting that fairness fundamentally represents a core software quality issue arising directly from performance disparities across sensitive user groups. Recognizing fairness explicitly as a software quality dimension yields practical benefits beyond ethical considerations, notably improved predictive performance for unprivileged groups, enhanced out-of-distribution generalization, and increased geographic transferability in real-world deployments. Nevertheless, existing bias mitigation methods face a critical dilemma: while pre-processing methods offer broad applicability across model types, they generally fall short in effectiveness compared to post-processing techniques. To overcome this challenge, we propose Correlation Tuning (CoT), a novel pre-processing approach designed to mitigate bias by adjusting data correlations. Specifically, CoT introduces the Phi-coefficient, an intuitive correlation measure, to systematically quantify correlation between sensitive attributes and labels, and employs multi-objective optimization to address the proxy biases. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that CoT increases the true positive rate of unprivileged groups by an average of 17.5% and reduces three key bias metrics, including statistical parity difference (SPD), average odds difference (AOD), and equal opportunity difference (EOD), by more than 50% on average. CoT outperforms state-of-the-art methods by three and ten percentage points in single attribute and multiple attributes scenarios, respectively. We will publicly release our experimental results and source code to facilitate future research.

AI.tldr: This paper positions software fairness as a software quality dimension beyond ethical concerns, and introduces Correlation Tuning (CoT), a pre-processing technique which outperforms state-of-the-art methods in reducing bias metrics and improving predictive performance for unprivileged groups.
AI.motivation: Traditional fairness approaches either have broad applicability but poor effectiveness (pre-processing) or high effectiveness but limited applicability (post-processing). The authors note that fairness is not only an ethical concern but a software quality issue, affecting performance across user groups and real-world deployment scenarios such as generalization and transferability.
AI.method: CoT is a pre-processing method that uses the Phi-coefficient to measure correlation between sensitive attributes and labels. It uses multi-objective optimization to mitigate proxy biases by adjusting data correlations.
AI.result: CoT outperforms existing methods by 3-10% in reducing bias metrics and improving true positive rates for unprivileged groups. Specifically, it increases the true positive rate by 17.5% on average and reduces SPD, AOD, and EOD by more than 50%.
AI.conclusion: CoT demonstrates practical effectiveness in addressing the fairness-quality trade-off, providing both broad applicability and high effectiveness. The paper advocates for viewing fairness as a software quality characteristic rather than only an ethical concern.

title: CosmoCore-Evo: Evolutionary Dream-Replay Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Code Generation
summary: Building on the affective dream-replay reinforcement learning framework of CosmoCore, we introduce CosmoCore-Evo, an extension that incorporates evolutionary algorithms to enhance adaptability and novelty in code generation tasks. Inspired by anthropological aspects of human evolution, such as natural selection and adaptation in early hominids, CosmoCore-Evo treats RL trajectories as ``genomes'' that undergo mutation and selection during the nocturnal replay phase. This mechanism allows agents to break free from trained patterns, fostering emergent behaviors and improved performance in distribution-shifted environments, such as changing APIs or novel libraries. We augment the Dream Queue with evolutionary operations, including mutation of high-fitness trajectories and enterprise-tuned fitness functions that incorporate efficiency, compliance, and scalability metrics. Evaluated on extended benchmarks including HumanEval variants with shifts, BigCodeBench, and a custom PySpark pipeline simulation, CosmoCore-Evo achieves up to 35% higher novelty in solutions and 25% faster adaptation compared to the original CosmoCore and baselines like PPO and REAMER. Ablations confirm the role of evolutionary components in bridging the sentient gap for LLM agents. Code for replication, including a toy simulation, is provided.

AI.tldr: CosmoCore-Evo introduces evolutionary algorithms to enhance adaptability and novelty in code generation by modifying Dream Queue with operations like mutation and fitness-based selection, leading to improved performance and faster adaptation in shifting environments.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from the need for improved adaptability and novelty in code generation tasks, particularly in environments where APIs and libraries evolve. By integrating evolutionary algorithms inspired by human evolution, the framework aims to break free from trained patterns and support emergent behaviors.
AI.method: The method involves extending the CosmoCore framework by treating RL trajectories as 'genomes' that undergo mutation and selection during a nocturnal replay phase. The Dream Queue is augmented with evolutionary operations, including mutation of high-fitness trajectories and enterprise-tuned fitness functions that emphasize efficiency, compliance, and scalability.
AI.result: CosmoCore-Evo demonstrated up to a 35% increase in solution novelty and a 25% faster adaptation rate compared to the original CosmoCore and baseline methods like PPO and REAMER. Ablation studies confirmed the significance of the evolutionary components in enhancing agent performance.
AI.conclusion: The integration of evolutionary algorithms in CosmoCore-Evo successfully bridges the sentient gap for LLM agents by fostering adaptability and innovation in code generation tasks. The results underscore the potential of evolutionary strategies in creating more robust and responsive agents in dynamic environments.

title: Multi-Agent LLM Committees for Autonomous Software Beta Testing
summary: Manual software beta testing is costly and time-consuming, while single-agent large language model (LLM) approaches suffer from hallucinations and inconsistent behavior. We propose a multi-agent committee framework in which diverse vision-enabled LLMs collaborate through a three-round voting protocol to reach consensus on testing actions. The framework combines model diversity, persona-driven behavioral variation, and visual user interface understanding to systematically explore web applications. Across 84 experimental runs with 9 testing personas and 4 scenarios, multi-agent committees achieve an 89.5 percent overall task success rate. Configurations with 2 to 4 agents reach 91.7 to 100 percent success, compared to 78.0 percent for single-agent baselines, yielding improvements of 13.7 to 22.0 percentage points. At the action level, the system attains a 93.1 percent success rate with a median per-action latency of 0.71 seconds, enabling real-time and continuous integration testing. Vision-enabled agents successfully identify user interface elements, with navigation and reporting achieving 100 percent success and form filling achieving 99.2 percent success. We evaluate the framework on WebShop and OWASP benchmarks, achieving 74.7 percent success on WebShop compared to a 50.1 percent published GPT-3 baseline, and 82.0 percent success on OWASP Juice Shop security testing with coverage of 8 of the 10 OWASP Top 10 vulnerability categories. Across 20 injected regressions, the committee achieves an F1 score of 0.91 for bug detection, compared to 0.78 for single-agent baselines. The open-source implementation enables reproducible research and practical deployment of LLM-based software testing in CI/CD pipelines.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent LLM framework with voting protocols outperforms single-agent models in automated web testing (89.5% success vs 78%) and security benchmarks, enabling real-time, reproducible testing in CI/CD pipelines.
AI.motivation: Manual software beta testing is costly and time-consuming, while single-agent LLMs suffer from hallucinations and inconsistent behavior, motivating the need for a collaborative, robust testing approach.
AI.method: A multi-agent committee framework with diverse vision-enabled LLMs collaborates through a three-round voting protocol, leveraging model diversity, persona-driven behavior, and visual UI understanding to explore web applications.
AI.result: The framework achieves 89.5% task success (91.7-100% for 2-4 agents) vs. 78% for single-agent baselines, 93.1% action success, and 82% OWASP security testing success, with 0.91 F1 score for bug detection.
AI.conclusion: The multi-agent committee framework demonstrates significant improvements in web application testing compared to single-agent LLMs, achieving high task success rates, effective bug detection, and compatibility with CI/CD pipelines.

title: Reflection-Driven Control for Trustworthy Code Agents
comment: Accepted to AAAI 2026 Workshop on Trust and Control in Agentic AI (TrustAgent)
summary: Contemporary large language model (LLM) agents are remarkably capable, but they still lack reliable safety controls and can produce unconstrained, unpredictable, and even actively harmful outputs. To address this, we introduce Reflection-Driven Control, a standardized and pluggable control module that can be seamlessly integrated into general agent architectures. Reflection-Driven Control elevates "self-reflection" from a post hoc patch into an explicit step in the agent's own reasoning process: during generation, the agent continuously runs an internal reflection loop that monitors and evaluates its own decision path. When potential risks are detected, the system retrieves relevant repair examples and secure coding guidelines from an evolving reflective memory, injecting these evidence-based constraints directly into subsequent reasoning steps. We instantiate Reflection-Driven Control in the setting of secure code generation and systematically evaluate it across eight classes of security-critical programming tasks. Empirical results show that Reflection-Driven Control substantially improves the security and policy compliance of generated code while largely preserving functional correctness, with minimal runtime and token overhead. Taken together, these findings indicate that Reflection-Driven Control is a practical path toward trustworthy AI coding agents: it enables designs that are simultaneously autonomous, safer by construction, and auditable.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Reflection-Driven Control, a safety mechanism for LLM agents during code generation by embedding an internal reflection loop that detects and mitigates risks through retrieval of secure coding guidelines. The system improves security and policy compliance with minimal overhead, offering a practical path toward trustworthy AI coding agents.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agents generate outputs that are unconstrained and unpredictable, leading to potentially harmful outcomes, especially in security-sensitive domains. The paper addresses this by seeking a standardized and reliable safety control to enhance trustworthiness without compromising functionality.
AI.method: The proposed method integrates a Reflection-Driven Control module into general agent architectures. This module incorporates an internal reflection loop to monitor reasoning paths and inject constraints by retrieving secure coding guidelines from a reflective memory during code generation, mitigating risks at runtime.
AI.result: The method improves security and policy compliance across eight classes of security-critical tasks while preserving functional correctness and incurring minimal runtime overhead.
AI.conclusion: Reflection-Driven Control is a practical framework for building secure, autonomous, auditable AI coding agents. It demonstrates a scalable path to align AI behavior with safety requirements, without requiring significant architectural changes.

title: AInsteinBench: Benchmarking Coding Agents on Scientific Repositories
summary: We introduce AInsteinBench, a large-scale benchmark for evaluating whether large language model (LLM) agents can operate as scientific computing development agents within real research software ecosystems. Unlike existing scientific reasoning benchmarks which focus on conceptual knowledge, or software engineering benchmarks that emphasize generic feature implementation and issue resolving, AInsteinBench evaluates models in end-to-end scientific development settings grounded in production-grade scientific repositories. The benchmark consists of tasks derived from maintainer-authored pull requests across six widely used scientific codebases, spanning quantum chemistry, quantum computing, molecular dynamics, numerical relativity, fluid dynamics, and cheminformatics. All benchmark tasks are carefully curated through multi-stage filtering and expert review to ensure scientific challenge, adequate test coverage, and well-calibrated difficulty. By leveraging evaluation in executable environments, scientifically meaningful failure modes, and test-driven verification, AInsteinBench measures a model's ability to move beyond surface-level code generation toward the core competencies required for computational scientific research.

AI.tldr: AInsteinBench is a large-scale benchmark to evaluate LLM agents in scientific software development.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks focus on conceptual knowledge or generic software tasks, while AInsteinBench addresses the gap by testing LLMs in real scientific development scenarios.
AI.method: AInsteinBench includes tasks from six scientific codebases, curated through a multi-stage process. Evaluation is in executable environments with test-driven verification.
AI.result: The benchmark provides tasks across various scientific domains with calibrated difficulty, ensuring scientific challenge and meaningful failure modes.
AI.conclusion: AInsteinBench enables evaluation of LLMs beyond code generation, focusing on core competencies in computational research.

title: Composition Theorems for f-Differential Privacy
summary: "f differential privacy" (fDP) is a recent definition for privacy privacy which can offer improved predictions of "privacy loss". It has been used to analyse specific privacy mechanisms, such as the popular Gaussian mechanism. In this paper we show how fDP's foundation in statistical hypothesis testing implies equivalence to the channel model of Quantitative Information Flow. We demonstrate this equivalence by a Galois connection between two partially ordered sets. This equivalence enables novel general composition theorems for fDP, supporting improved analysis for complex privacy designs.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes the equivalence between fDP (a privacy framework based on hypothesis testing) and QIF (Quantitative Information Flow) via Galois connections, enabling new composition theorems for analyzing privacy mechanisms.
AI.motivation: While fDP improves privacy loss predictions, its connection to existing information-theoretic frameworks (like QIF) was unexplored, limiting deeper analytical insights. Bridging this gap could strengthen privacy analysis and design.
AI.method: The authors demonstrate an equivalence between fDP and QIF by constructing a Galois connection between two partially ordered sets (posets). This mathematical structure reveals an inherent relationship between their definitions, enabling the derivation of composition properties.
AI.result: A formal equivalence between fDP and QIF is proven, along with novel composition theorems for fDP. These theorems allow better analysis of combined privacy mechanisms, such as parallel and sequential applications, beyond traditional results.
AI.conclusion: By linking fDP to QIF through ordered structure theory, the paper provides a theoretical foundation for leveraging QIF's tools within fDP. This opens avenues for more robust privacy design, particularly in complex systems requiring rigorous compositional analysis.

title: What Makes a GitHub Issue Ready for Copilot?
summary: AI-agents help developers in different coding tasks, such as developing new features, fixing bugs, and reviewing code. Developers can write a Github issue and assign it to an AI-agent like Copilot for implementation. Based on the issue and its related discussion, the AI-agent performs a plan for the implementation, and executes it. However, the performance of AI-agents and LLMs heavily depends on the input they receive. For instance, a GitHub issue that is unclear or not well scoped might not lead to a successful implementation that will eventually be merged. GitHub Copilot provides a set of best practice recommendations that are limited and high-level. In this paper, we build a set of 32 detailed criteria that we leverage to measure the quality of GitHub issues to make them suitable for AI-agents. We compare the GitHub issues that lead to a merged pull request versus closed pull request. Then, we build an interpretable machine learning model to predict the likelihood of a GitHub issue resulting in a merged pull request. We observe that pull requests that end up being merged are those originating from issues that are shorter, well scoped, with clear guidance and hints about the relevant artifacts for an issue, and with guidance on how to perform the implementation. Issues with external references including configuration, context setup, dependencies or external APIs are associated with lower merge rates. We built an interpretable machine learning model to help users identify how to improve a GitHub issue to increase the chances of the issue resulting in a merged pull request by Copilot. Our model has a median AUC of 72\%. Our results shed light on quality metrics relevant for writing GitHub issues and motivate future studies further investigate the writing of GitHub issues as a first-class software engineering activity in the era of AI-teammates.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces 32 detailed criteria for evaluating the quality of GitHub issues to enhance their effectiveness when assigned to AI-agents like Copilot. An interpretable machine learning model is built to predict the likelihood of a GitHub issue leading to a merged pull request. By analyzing successful versus unsuccessful issues, the study shows that clarity, brevity, and specific guidance increase merge rates while external dependencies decrease them. The model achieves a median AUC of 72%, offering practical insights into writing effective GitHub issues.
AI.motivation: The performance of AI-agents during coding tasks, like implementing features using GitHub issues, depends heavily on the clarity and quality of the issues provided by developers. Current best practices are limited and do not offer sufficient insight into what makes an issue suitable for an AI agent, possibly leading to failed implementations.
AI.method: The researchers created 32 quality criteria for GitHub issues based on a comparison of successful (merged) and unsuccessful (closed) cases. They then built an interpretable machine learning model to analyze these criteria and predict whether an issue would result in a merged pull request.
AI.result: The analysis found that short, well-scoped issues with clear implementation guidance and no external dependencies are more likely to result in a merged pull request. The model built from the criteria achieved a median AUC of 72% in its predictions.
AI.conclusion: The study underscores the importance of GitHub issue quality when working with AI agents, offering actionable criteria to help developers write effective issues. It also highlights that writing GitHub issues should be considered a first-class software engineering activity as AI teammates become more prevalent.

title: Power Side-Channel Analysis of the CVA6 RISC-V Core at the RTL Level Using VeriSide
summary: Security in modern RISC-V processors demands more than functional correctness: It requires resilience to side-channel attacks. This paper evaluates the vulnerability of the side channel of the CVA6 RISC-V core by analyzing software-based AES encryption uses an RTL-level power profiling framework called VeriSide. This work represents that this design's Correlation Power Analysis (CPA) reveals significant leakage, enabling key recovery. These findings underscore the importance of early-stage RTL assessments in shaping future secure RISC-V designs.

AI.tldr: This paper highlights the vulnerability of CVA6 RISC-V core to side-channel attacks through CPA analysis using VeriSide framework.
AI.motivation: Modern RISC-V processors require resilience to side-channel attacks, which demands assessment beyond functional correctness.
AI.method: The study uses VeriSide, an RTL-level power profiling framework, to evaluate the side-channel vulnerability of CVA6 RISC-V core via software-based AES encryption and Correlation Power Analysis (CPA).
AI.result: The analysis reveals significant power leakage in CVA6 RISC-V core that could enable key recovery through CPA.
AI.conclusion: The study emphasizes the role of early-stage RTL evaluations in developing secure RISC-V processors against side-channel attacks.

title: Cerberus: Multi-Agent Reasoning and Coverage-Guided Exploration for Static Detection of Runtime Errors
summary: In several software development scenarios, it is desirable to detect runtime errors and exceptions in code snippets without actual execution. A typical example is to detect runtime exceptions in online code snippets before integrating them into a codebase. In this paper, we propose Cerberus, a novel predictive, execution-free coverage-guided testing framework. Cerberus uses LLMs to generate the inputs that trigger runtime errors and to perform code coverage prediction and error detection without code execution. With a two-phase feedback loop, Cerberus first aims to both increasing code coverage and detecting runtime errors, then shifts to focus only detecting runtime errors when the coverage reaches 100% or its maximum, enabling it to perform better than prompting the LLMs for both purposes. Our empirical evaluation demonstrates that Cerberus performs better than conventional and learning-based testing frameworks for (in)complete code snippets by generating high-coverage test cases more efficiently, leading to the discovery of more runtime errors.

AI.tldr: Cerberus is a predictive, execution-free testing framework using LLMs to generate test inputs for detecting runtime errors and increasing code coverage without executing the code. It outperforms existing methods in efficiency and error detection.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the need to detect runtime errors in code snippets without execution, which is crucial for integrating online code into a codebase safely and efficiently.
AI.method: Cerberus employs a two-phase feedback loop with LLMs. It initially focuses on increasing code coverage and detecting runtime errors, then shifts to exclusively error detection once maximum coverage is achieved. This approach leverages LLMs for generating test inputs and predicting coverage and errors without code execution.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows that Cerberus outperforms conventional and learning-based testing frameworks by generating high-coverage test cases more efficiently, leading to the discovery of more runtime errors in both complete and incomplete code snippets.
AI.conclusion: Cerberus provides an effective solution for execution-free runtime error detection and coverage-guided testing, proving superior in efficiency and effectiveness compared to existing methods.

title: Satellite Cybersecurity Across Orbital Altitudes: Analyzing Ground-Based Threats to LEO, MEO, and GEO
summary: The rapid proliferation of satellite constellations, particularly in Low Earth Orbit (LEO), has fundamentally altered the global space infrastructure, shifting the risk landscape from purely kinetic collisions to complex cyber-physical threats. While traditional safety frameworks focus on debris mitigation, ground-based adversaries increasingly exploit radio-frequency links, supply chain vulnerabilities, and software update pathways to degrade space assets. This paper presents a comparative analysis of satellite cybersecurity across LEO, Medium Earth Orbit (MEO), and Geostationary Earth Orbit (GEO) regimes. By synthesizing data from 60 publicly documented security incidents with key vulnerability proxies--including Telemetry, Tracking, and Command (TT&C) anomalies, encryption weaknesses, and environmental stressors--we characterize how orbital altitude dictates attack feasibility and impact. Our evaluation reveals distinct threat profiles: GEO systems are predominantly targeted via high-frequency uplink exposure, whereas LEO constellations face unique risks stemming from limited power budgets, hardware constraints, and susceptibility to thermal and radiation-induced faults. We further bridge the gap between security and sustainability, arguing that unmitigated cyber vulnerabilities accelerate hardware obsolescence and debris accumulation, undermining efforts toward carbon-neutral space operations. The results demonstrate that weak encryption and command path irregularities are the most consistent predictors of adversarial success across all orbits.

AI.tldr: Satellite cybersecurity threats differ drastically by orbit, with encryption and command flaws being universally critical; integrated solutions are required to link cyber resilience to space sustainability.
AI.motivation: The proliferation of LEO satellite constellations has shifted space risks from kinetic collisions to complex cyber-physical threats. Traditional debris-focused frameworks neglect vulnerabilities in radio-frequency links, supply chains, and software updates, necessitating a reevaluation of orbit-specific cybersecurity strategies.
AI.method: The study conducts a comparative analysis of satellite cybersecurity across LEO, MEO, and GEO regimes by synthesizing data from 60 publicly documented security incidents. Key vulnerability proxies included TT&C anomalies, encryption weaknesses, and environmental stressors, with statistical evaluation of attack feasibility and impact.
AI.result: Distinct threat profiles emerge for each orbit: GEO is vulnerable to high-frequency uplink attacks, LEO faces risks from power/hardware constraints and environmental faults, and weak encryption/command irregularities predict adversarial success across all orbits. Cyber vulnerabilities directly impact sustainability via accelerated hardware depreciation.
AI.conclusion: The paper argues that unmitigated cyber vulnerabilities in satellite systems accelerate hardware obsolescence and debris accumulation, undermining sustainable carbon-neutral space operations. It emphasizes the need for integrated cybersecurity measures addressing orbit-specific risks.

title: Fuzzwise: Intelligent Initial Corpus Generation for Fuzzing
summary: In mutation-based greybox fuzzing, generating high-quality input seeds for the initial corpus is essential for effective fuzzing. Rather than conducting separate phases for generating a large corpus and subsequently minimizing it, we propose FuzzWise which integrates them into one process to generate the optimal initial corpus of seeds (ICS). FuzzWise leverages a multi-agent framework based on Large Language Models (LLMs). The first LLM agent generates test cases for the target program. The second LLM agent, which functions as a predictive code coverage module, assesses whether each generated test case will enhance the overall coverage of the current corpus. The streamlined process allows each newly generated test seed to be immediately evaluated for its contribution to the overall coverage. FuzzWise employs a predictive approach using an LLM and eliminates the need for actual execution, saving computational resources and time, particularly in scenarios where the execution is not desirable or even impossible. Our empirical evaluation demonstrates that FuzzWise generates significantly fewer test cases than baseline methods. Despite the lower number of test cases, FuzzWise achieves high code coverage and triggers more runtime errors compared to the baselines. Moreover, it is more time-efficient and coverage-efficient in producing an initial corpus catching more errors.

AI.tldr: FuzzWise is a mutation-based greybox fuzzing method that integrates initial corpus generation and minimization using a multi-agent framework with Large Language Models (LLMs). It reduces test cases while improving code coverage and error detection efficiency through predictive coverage analysis without execution.
AI.motivation: Traditional greybox fuzzing separately generates and minimizes test corpora, leading to time inefficiency. FuzzWise addresses this by unifying these processes to optimize the initial corpus and reduce computational overhead, especially in scenarios where execution is impractical.
AI.method: FuzzWise employs two LLM agents: one generates test cases for the target program, while the second functions as a predictive code coverage module. The second agent evaluates coverage improvement without actual execution, enabling real-time optimization of the test corpus.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show FuzzWise generates significantly fewer test cases than baselines while achieving higher code coverage and triggering more runtime errors. It is more time-efficient and coverage-efficient in error detection.
AI.conclusion: FuzzWise successfully integrates corpus generation and minimization using LLMs, offering a time- and coverage-efficient approach for creating optimal seed corpora in greybox fuzzing, particularly in execution-restricted scenarios.

title: Key Length-Oriented Classification of Lightweight Cryptographic Algorithms for IoT Security
summary: The successful deployment of the Internet of Things (IoT) applications relies heavily on their robust security, and lightweight cryptography is considered an emerging solution in this context. While existing surveys have been examining lightweight cryptographic techniques from the perspective of hardware and software implementations or performance evaluation, there is a significant gap in addressing different security aspects specific to the IoT environment. This study aims to bridge this gap. This research presents a thorough survey focused on the security evaluation of symmetric lightweight ciphers commonly used in IoT systems. The objective of this study is to provide a holistic understanding of lightweight ciphers, emphasizing their security strength, which is an essential consideration for real-time and resource-constrained applications. Furthermore, we propose two taxonomies: one for classifying IoT applications based on their inherent characteristics, and another for evaluating security levels based on key size. Our findings indicate that key size is a critical parameter in the security of lightweight ciphers. Ciphers employing keys shorter than 128 bits are considered less secure or even insecure for protecting sensitive data

AI.tldr: This paper surveys lightweight ciphers for IoT, emphasizes key size as a security benchmark, and introduces taxonomies for IoT application classification and security evaluation. Ciphers with keys <128 bits are flagged as insecure for sensitive data.
AI.motivation: Existing surveys on lightweight cryptography focus on hardware/software performance or implementations, but overlook IoT-specific security challenges. This study bridges that gap by prioritizing security evaluation for resource-constrained IoT environments.
AI.method: The research conducts a comprehensive survey of security evaluations for symmetric lightweight ciphers. It introduces two taxonomies: one for categorizing IoT applications based on characteristics, and another for assessing security levels using key size as a parameter.
AI.result: Analysis reveals that key sizes below 128 bits render lightweight ciphers insecure. The proposed taxonomies provide a framework to assess security requirements and classify IoT applications based on inherent characteristics and security needs.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that key size is a critical factor in the security of lightweight ciphers for IoT systems, with ciphers using keys shorter than 128 bits deemed insufficient for sensitive data protection. The proposed taxonomies aid in evaluating security levels and classifying IoT applications.

title: Code Clone Refactoring in C# with Lambda Expressions
comment: 8 pages, World Symposium on Software Engineering (WSSE 2025)
summary:
"Extract Method" refactoring is a technique for consolidating code clones. Parameterization approaches are used to extract a single method from multiple code clones that contain differences. This approach parameterizes expressions and behaviors within a method. In particular, behavior parameterization has been extensively studied in Java programs, but little research has been conducted on other programming languages.
  Lambda expressions can be used to parameterize behaviors, but the specifications of each programming language significantly affect the applicability of this technique. Therefore, the optimal "Extract Method" approach may vary depending on the programming language.
  In this study, we propose a C#-specific technique that uses lambda expressions to analyze and consolidate code clones. We evaluated our proposed method by applying it to code clones detected by the NiCad clone detector and measuring how many of them could be successfully consolidated.
  In total, 2,217 clone pairs from 22 projects were included in our evaluation. For the clone pairs determined to be refactorable, we also attempted refactoring actually. The proposed approach determined that 35.0% of all clone pairs were suitable for refactoring. Among these, 28.9% were successfully refactored.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a C#-specific 'Extract Method' refactoring technique using lambda expressions to consolidate code clones and evaluates its effectiveness on clone pairs detected by NiCad.
AI.motivation: The study is motivated by the lack of research on behavior parameterization techniques in languages other than Java, and the need for optimal 'Extract Method' refactoring that considers language-specific characteristics.
AI.method: The authors propose a C#-specific approach for 'Extract Method' refactoring that uses lambda expressions, analyze and consolidate code clones. They evaluate the method by applying it to 2,217 clone pairs from 22 projects, identifying refactorable pairs and attempting actual refactoring.
AI.result: The proposed approach determined that 35.0% of all clone pairs were suitable for refactoring. Among these, 28.9% were successfully refactored using the method.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that using lambda expressions in a C#-specific manner can help analyze and consolidate code clones, with the proposed approach showing a moderate success rate in determining and performing refactorable clone pairs.

title: The Imitation Game: Using Large Language Models as Chatbots to Combat Chat-Based Cybercrimes
summary:
Chat-based cybercrime has emerged as a pervasive threat, with attackers leveraging real-time messaging platforms to conduct scams that rely on trust-building, deception, and psychological manipulation. Traditional defense mechanisms, which operate on static rules or shallow content filters, struggle to identify these conversational threats, especially when attackers use multimedia obfuscation and context-aware dialogue.
  In this work, we ask a provocative question inspired by the classic Imitation Game: Can machines convincingly pose as human victims to turn deception against cybercriminals? We present LURE (LLM-based User Response Engagement), the first system to deploy Large Language Models (LLMs) as active agents, not as passive classifiers, embedded within adversarial chat environments.
  LURE combines automated discovery, adversarial interaction, and OCR-based analysis of image-embedded payment data. Applied to the setting of illicit video chat scams on Telegram, our system engaged 53 actors across 98 groups. In over 56 percent of interactions, the LLM maintained multi-round conversations without being noticed as a bot, effectively "winning" the imitation game. Our findings reveal key behavioral patterns in scam operations, such as payment flows, upselling strategies, and platform migration tactics.

AI.tldr: A new system named LURE uses large language models to act as human chat participants in order to interact with cybercriminals and gain insight into their techniques, successfully remaining undetected in over half of the interactions.
AI.motivation: Traditional cybersecurity measures fail to detect conversational cybercrimes such as chat-based scams that rely on trust and psychological manipulation. These methods use real-time messaging platforms and often include multimedia obfuscation, making them difficult for static filters to identify.
AI.method: The study introduces LURE (LLM-based User Response Engagement), which employs large language models as active agents within adversarial chat environments. It uses automated discovery to find scam groups, then engages in conversations with criminals, and utilizes OCR to analyze image-embedded payment data. This allows LURE to maintain realistic conversations and gather data on scam operations.
AI.result: LURE was applied to Telegram-based video chat scams and interacted with 53 actors across 98 groups. In over 56% of interactions, the LLM maintained multi-round conversations without being detected as a bot. The system successfully gathered insights into scam operations, including payment flows, upselling strategies, and platform migration tactics.
AI.conclusion: LURE demonstrates the effectiveness of using large language models as active agents in adversarial chat environments to study and counteract cybercriminal activities. The system's ability to convincingly impersonate a human and remain undetected provides valuable insights into the tactics used by cybercriminals and highlights the potential of LLMs for proactive cybersecurity measures.

title: XTrace: A Non-Invasive Dynamic Tracing Framework for Android Applications in Production
summary: As the complexity of mobile applications grows exponentially and the fragmentation of user device environments intensifies, ensuring online application stability faces unprecedented challenges. Traditional methods, such as static logging and post-crash analysis, lack real-time contextual information, rendering them ineffective against "ghost bugs" that only manifest in specific scenarios. This highlights an urgent need for dynamic runtime observability: intercepting and tracing arbitrary methods in production without requiring an app release. We propose XTrace, a novel dynamic tracing framework. XTrace introduces a new paradigm of non-invasive proxying, which avoids direct modification of the virtual machine's underlying data structures. It achieves high-performance method interception by leveraging and optimizing the highly stable, built-in instrumentation mechanism of the Android ART virtual machine. Evaluated in a ByteDance application with hundreds of millions of daily active users, XTrace demonstrated production-grade stability and performance. Large-scale online A/B experiments confirmed its stability, showing no statistically significant impact (p > 0.05) on Crash User Rate or ANR rate, while maintaining minimal overhead (<7 ms startup latency, <0.01 ms per-method call) and broad compatibility (Android 5.0-15+). Critically, XTrace diagnosed over 11 severe online crashes and multiple performance bottlenecks, improving root-cause localization efficiency by over 90%. This confirms XTrace provides a production-grade solution that reconciles the long-standing conflict between stability and comprehensive coverage in Android dynamic tracing.

AI.tldr: XTrace is a new dynamic tracing framework for Android that provides real-time, non-invasive method interception and tracing to address complex bugs in production environments while maintaining stability and minimal performance impact.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to tackle smartphone application instability, especially due to elusive bugs (ghost bugs) that only occur in specific device and runtime scenarios and are difficult to detect with traditional methods like static logging or post-crash analysis.
AI.method: XTrace employs a novel non-invasive proxying technique and leverages the Android ART virtual machine's built-in instrumentation for fast and stable tracing. It enables arbitrary method interception at runtime without altering ART's internal data structures.
AI.result: XTrace was evaluated in a major application with a massive user base at ByteDance, demonstrating high stability and performance. A/B testing showed no significant impact on crash or ANR rates. It detected over 11 critical crashes and multiple performance bottlenecks with minimal overhead.
AI.conclusion: XTrace successfully reconciles the trade-off between comprehensive runtime coverage and system stability in Android apps, offering a production-ready solution for dynamic tracing with non-invasive, high-performance capabilities.

title: Security Risks Introduced by Weak Authentication in Smart Home IoT Systems
summary: Smart home IoT systems rely on authentication mechanisms to ensure that only authorized entities can control devices and access sensitive functionality. In practice, these mechanisms must balance security with usability, often favoring persistent connectivity and minimal user interaction. This paper presents an empirical analysis of authentication enforcement in deployed smart home IoT devices, focusing on how authentication state is established, reused, and validated during normal operation and under routine network conditions. A set of widely deployed consumer devices, including smart plugs, lighting devices, cameras, and a hub based ecosystem, was evaluated in a controlled residential environment using passive network measurement and controlled interaction through official mobile applications. Authentication behavior was examined during initial pairing, over extended periods of operation, after common network changes, and under replay attempts from a different local network host. The results show that authentication state established during pairing is consistently reused across control actions, persists for extended periods without explicit expiration, and remains valid after network events such as reconnection, address reassignment, and router reboot. Replay experiments demonstrate that previously observed authentication artifacts can often be reused to issue control commands from another host on the same local network with high success rates. These behaviors were observed across multiple device categories and ecosystems. The findings indicate that current smart home IoT authentication mechanisms rely on long lived trust relationships with limited binding to session freshness, network context, or controller identity.

AI.tldr: Smart home IoT devices reuse authentication credentials indefinitely and allow replay attacks, exposing serious security vulnerabilities due to weak trust binding.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the need to understand how authentication is practically enforced in smart home IoT systems, particularly how these systems balance security requirements with usability demands like persistent connectivity and minimal user interaction.
AI.method: The researchers evaluated a set of widely deployed consumer IoT devices (smart plugs, lighting, cameras, and hubs) in a controlled residential environment using passive network monitoring, mobile app interactions, and replay experiments to analyze authentication behavior during pairing, extended use, network events (e.g., reconnection, address reassignment), and cross-host replay attempts.
AI.result: Authentication state persistently reuses credentials for extended periods without expiration, survives network disruptions (e.g., reboots, address changes), and can be successfully replayed from unauthorized hosts on the same network with high success rates across multiple device categories and ecosystems.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that current smart home IoT authentication mechanisms prioritize usability over security by relying on long-lived trust relationships with insufficient binding to session freshness, network context, or controller identity, creating significant security risks.

title: Co-Evolution of Types and Dependencies: Towards Repository-Level Type Inference for Python Code
summary: Python's dynamic typing mechanism, while promoting flexibility, is a significant source of runtime type errors that plague large-scale software, which inspires the automatic type inference techniques. Existing type inference tools have achieved advances in type inference within isolated code snippets. However, repository-level type inference remains a significant challenge, primarily due to the complex inter-procedural dependencies that are difficult to model and resolve. To fill this gap, we present \methodName, a novel approach based on LLMs that achieves repository-level type inference through the co-evolution of types and dependencies. \methodName~constructs an Entity Dependency Graph (EDG) to model the objects and type dependencies across the repository. During the inference process, it iteratively refines types and dependencies in EDG for accurate type inference. Our key innovations are: (1) an EDG model designed to capture repository-level type dependencies; (2) an iterative type inference approach where types and dependencies co-evolve in each iteration; and (3) a type-checker-in-the-loop strategy that validates and corrects inferences on-the-fly, thereby reducing error propagation. When evaluated on 12 complex Python repositories, \methodName~significantly outperformed prior works, achieving a \textit{TypeSim} score of 0.89 and a \textit{TypeExact} score of 0.84, representing a 27\% and 40\% relative improvement over the strongest baseline. More importantly, \methodName~removed new type errors introduced by the tool by 92.7\%. This demonstrates a significant leap towards automated, reliable type annotation for real-world Python development.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces \methodName, a novel LLM-based approach for repository-level type inference in Python, which addresses challenges posed by inter-procedural dependencies. By employing an Entity Dependency Graph and an iterative refinement process, it significantly outperforms prior works in type accuracy and error reduction.
AI.motivation: Python's dynamic typing, while flexible, introduces runtime type errors in large-scale software. Current type inference tools are limited to isolated code snippets, failing to handle repository-level complexities due to inter-procedural dependencies.
AI.method: \methodName constructs an Entity Dependency Graph (EDG) to model type dependencies across a repository. It utilizes an iterative process where types and dependencies evolve together, enhanced by a type-checker-in-the-loop strategy to validate and correct inferences.
AI.result: Evaluations on 12 Python repositories showed \methodName achieved a 27% and 40% improvement in TypeSim and TypeExact scores over the best baseline, respectively, while reducing new type errors by 92.7%.
AI.conclusion: The proposed \methodName offers a substantial advancement in automated, reliable type annotation for real-world Python development by effectively managing complex, repository-level type dependencies.

title: A Systematic Review of Technical Defenses Against Software-Based Cheating in Online Multiplayer Games
summary: This systematic literature review surveys technical defenses against software-based cheating in online multiplayer games. Categorizing existing approach-es into server-side detection, client-side anti-tamper, kernel-level anti-cheat drivers, and hardware-assisted TEEs. Each category is evaluated in terms of detection effectiveness, perfor-mance overhead, privacy im-pact, and scalability. The analy-sis highlights key trade-offs, particularly between the high visibility of kernel-level solutions and their privacy and stability risks, versus the low intrusive-ness but limited insight of server-side methods. Overall, the re-view emphasizes the ongoing arms race with cheaters and the need for robust, adversary-resistant anti-cheat designs.

AI.tldr: This review categorizes and evaluates anti-cheat techniques for online games, revealing trade-offs in effectiveness, privacy, and stability, and stressing the need for robust, evolving defenses.
AI.motivation: The motivation lies in addressing the persistent challenge of software-based cheating in online multiplayer games, where evolving cheating methods necessitate a comprehensive evaluation of existing countermeasures to guide future research and development.
AI.method: The study employs a systematic literature review to categorize technical defenses into server-side detection, client-side anti-tamper, kernel-level anti-cheat drivers, and hardware-assisted TEEs, evaluating them across detection effectiveness, performance overhead, privacy impact, and scalability.
AI.result: The analysis highlights critical trade-offs between high-visibility kernel-level solutions (with privacy/stability risks) and less intrusive server-side approaches (with limited efficacy), while underscoring the dynamic evolution of cheating tactics.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that there is an ongoing arms race between anti-cheat mechanisms and cheaters, emphasizing the need for robust, adversary-resistant designs that balance effectiveness, privacy, and system stability.

title: How Do Agents Perform Code Optimization? An Empirical Study
summary: Performance optimization is a critical yet challenging aspect of software development, often requiring a deep understanding of system behavior, algorithmic tradeoffs, and careful code modifications. Although recent advances in AI coding agents have accelerated code generation and bug fixing, little is known about how these agents perform on real-world performance optimization tasks. We present the first empirical study comparing agent- and human-authored performance optimization commits, analyzing 324 agent-generated and 83 human-authored PRs from the AIDev dataset across adoption, maintainability, optimization patterns, and validation practices. We find that AI-authored performance PRs are less likely to include explicit performance validation than human-authored PRs (45.7\% vs. 63.6\%, $p=0.007$). In addition, AI-authored PRs largely use the same optimization patterns as humans. We further discuss limitations and opportunities for advancing agentic code optimization.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes 324 AI-authored and 83 human-authored performance optimization commits, finding that AI PRs are less likely to include performance validation and use similar optimization patterns as humans.
AI.motivation: There is a gap in understanding how AI coding agents perform in real-world performance optimization tasks, despite their effectiveness in code generation and bug fixing.
AI.method: The authors conduct an empirical study using the AIDev dataset, comparing adoption, maintainability, optimization patterns, and validation practices in agent- and human-authored performance optimization commits.
AI.result: AI-authored performance PRs show a significantly lower rate of explicit performance validation (45.7%) compared to human-authored (63.6%). Additionally, AI commits use optimization patterns similar to humans.
AI.conclusion: The paper highlights limitations in AI agent performance optimization, such as reduced validation, and suggests opportunities for improvement in agentic code optimization.

title: LLM-Driven Feature-Level Adversarial Attacks on Android Malware Detectors
summary: The rapid growth in both the scale and complexity of Android malware has driven the widespread adoption of machine learning (ML) techniques for scalable and accurate malware detection. Despite their effectiveness, these models remain vulnerable to adversarial attacks that introduce carefully crafted feature-level perturbations to evade detection while preserving malicious functionality. In this paper, we present LAMLAD, a novel adversarial attack framework that exploits the generative and reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) to bypass ML-based Android malware classifiers. LAMLAD employs a dual-agent architecture composed of an LLM manipulator, which generates realistic and functionality-preserving feature perturbations, and an LLM analyzer, which guides the perturbation process toward successful evasion. To improve efficiency and contextual awareness, LAMLAD integrates retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) into the LLM pipeline. Focusing on Drebin-style feature representations, LAMLAD enables stealthy and high-confidence attacks against widely deployed Android malware detection systems. We evaluate LAMLAD against three representative ML-based Android malware detectors and compare its performance with two state-of-the-art adversarial attack methods. Experimental results demonstrate that LAMLAD achieves an attack success rate (ASR) of up to 97%, requiring on average only three attempts per adversarial sample, highlighting its effectiveness, efficiency, and adaptability in practical adversarial settings. Furthermore, we propose an adversarial training-based defense strategy that reduces the ASR by more than 30% on average, significantly enhancing model robustness against LAMLAD-style attacks.

AI.tldr: LAMLAD: Large language models used to attack ML-based Android malware detectors with high success rate.
AI.motivation: Android malware's scale and complexity necessitate ML detection, but these models are vulnerable to adversarial attacks. Existing methods lack efficiency and functional preservation.
AI.method: LAMLAD uses a dual-agent LLM architecture with a manipulator for perturbations and an analyzer for evasion, enhanced by RAG for efficiency and context.
AI.result: LAMLAD achieved 97% ASR with three attempts/sample against ML malware detectors; adversarial training reduced ASR by 30%.
AI.conclusion: LAMLAD demonstrates strong adversarial attacks using LLMs, while proposed defense mitigates risks, highlighting LLMs in adversarial contexts.

title: The State of the SBOM Tool Ecosystems: A Comparative Analysis of SPDX and CycloneDX
summary: A Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) provides transparency by documenting software component metadata and dependencies. However, SBOM adoption depends on tool ecosystems. With two dominant formats: SPDX and CycloneDX - the ecosystems vary significantly in maturity, tool support, and community engagement. We conduct a quantitative comparison of use cases for 170 publicly advertised SBOM tools, identifying enhancement areas for each format. We compare health metrics of both ecosystems (171 CycloneDX versus 470 SPDX tools) to evaluate robustness and maturity. We quantitatively compare 36,990 issue reports from open-source tools to identify challenges and development opportunities. Finally, we investigate the top 250 open-source projects using each tool ecosystem and compare their health metrics. Our findings reveal distinct characteristics: projects using CycloneDX tools demonstrate higher developer engagement and certain health indicators, while SPDX tools benefit from a more mature ecosystem with broader tool availability and established industry adoption. This research provides insights for developers, contributors, and practitioners regarding complementary strengths of these ecosystems and identifies opportunities for mutual enhancement.

AI.tldr: This paper compares SPDX and CycloneDX SBOM ecosystems quantitatively, revealing strengths (developer engagement vs. maturity), challenges, and opportunities for collaboration.
AI.motivation: SBOM adoption hinges on tool ecosystems, yet the two dominant formats (SPDX and CycloneDX) exhibit significant differences in maturity, tool support, and community engagement.
AI.method: Quantitative analysis of 170 SBOM tools (171 CycloneDX vs. 470 SPDX), 36,990 issue reports, and health metrics of 250 projects per ecosystem to evaluate robustness, maturity, and development challenges.
AI.result: CycloneDX tools show higher developer engagement and specific health indicators, while SPDX tools benefit from a more mature ecosystem with broader adoption. Challenges include varying ecosystem robustness and development opportunities.
AI.conclusion: The study provides insights into the complementary strengths of SPDX and CycloneDX ecosystems, highlighting areas for mutual enhancement and guiding developers and practitioners in selecting or improving toolchains.

title: GoldenFuzz: Generative Golden Reference Hardware Fuzzing
summary:
Modern hardware systems, driven by demands for high performance and application-specific functionality, have grown increasingly complex, introducing large surfaces for bugs and security-critical vulnerabilities. Fuzzing has emerged as a scalable solution for discovering such flaws. Yet, existing hardware fuzzers suffer from limited semantic awareness, inefficient test refinement, and high computational overhead due to reliance on slow device simulation.
  In this paper, we present GoldenFuzz, a novel two-stage hardware fuzzing framework that partially decouples test case refinement from coverage and vulnerability exploration. GoldenFuzz leverages a fast, ISA-compliant Golden Reference Model (GRM) as a ``digital twin'' of the Device Under Test (DUT). It fuzzes the GRM first, enabling rapid, low-cost test case refinement, accelerating deep architectural exploration and vulnerability discovery on DUT. During the fuzzing pipeline, GoldenFuzz iteratively constructs test cases by concatenating carefully chosen instruction blocks that balance the subtle inter- and intra-instructions quality. A feedback-driven mechanism leveraging insights from both high- and low-coverage samples further enhances GoldenFuzz's capability in hardware state exploration. Our evaluation of three RISC-V processors, RocketChip, BOOM, and CVA6, demonstrates that GoldenFuzz significantly outperforms existing fuzzers in achieving the highest coverage with minimal test case length and computational overhead. GoldenFuzz uncovers all known vulnerabilities and discovers five new ones, four of which are classified as highly severe with CVSS v3 severity scores exceeding seven out of ten. It also identifies two previously unknown vulnerabilities in the commercial BA51-H core extension.

AI.tldr: GoldenFuzz is a two-stage fuzzer for hardware, using a fast reference model for quick test case refinement before DUT testing, resulting in higher coverage and efficiency, and finding numerous vulnerabilities.
AI.motivation: Existing hardware fuzzers suffer from limited semantic awareness, inefficient test refinement, and high computational overhead due to slow simulation.
AI.method: GoldenFuzz uses a two-stage approach with a fast, ISA-compliant Golden Reference Model (GRM) for initial fuzzing and test case refinement, followed by DUT testing. It employs a feedback-driven mechanism with coverage insights for better state exploration.
AI.result: GoldenFuzz outperforms existing fuzzers on three RISC-V processors (RocketChip, BOOM, CVA6), achieving the highest coverage with minimal test case length and overhead. It discovers all known and five new vulnerabilities, four classified as highly severe (CVSS v3 score >7).
AI.conclusion: GoldenFuzz is a novel two-stage fuzzer with a GRM digital twin, enabling efficient and effective vulnerability discovery in hardware with reduced computational cost. It significantly outperforms existing fuzzers and uncovers highly critical vulnerabilities in commercial and open-source hardware.

title: A Story About Cohesion and Separation: Label-Free Metric for Log Parser Evaluation
comment: Accepted at the 33rd IEEE International Conference on Software Analysis, Evolution and Reengineering (SANER), Research Papers track, 2026
summary: Log parsing converts log messages into structured event templates, allowing for automated log analysis and reducing manual inspection effort. To select the most compatible parser for a specific system, multiple evaluation metrics are commonly used for performance comparisons. However, existing evaluation metrics heavily rely on labeled log data, which limits prior studies to a fixed set of datasets and hinders parser evaluations and selections in the industry. Further, we discovered that different versions of ground-truth used in existing studies can lead to inconsistent performance conclusions. Motivated by these challenges, we propose a novel label-free template-level metric, PMSS (parser medoid silhouette score), to evaluate log parser performance. PMSS evaluates both parser grouping and template quality with medoid silhouette analysis and Levenshtein distance within a near-linear time complexity in general. To understand its relationship with label-based template-level metrics, FGA and FTA, we compared their evaluation outcomes for six log parsers on the standard corrected Loghub 2.0 dataset. Our results indicate that log parsers achieving the highest PMSS or FGA exhibit comparable performance, differing by only 2.1% on average in terms of the FGA score; the difference is 9.8% for FTA. PMSS is also significantly (p<1e-8) and positively correlated to both FGA and FTA: the Spearman's rho correlation coefficient of PMSS-FGA and PMSS-FTA are respectively 0.648 and 0.587, close to the coefficient between FGA and FTA (0.670). We further extended our discussion on how to interpret the conclusions from different metrics, identifying challenges in using PMSS, and provided guidelines on conducting parser selections with our metric. PMSS provides a valuable evaluation alternative when ground-truths are inconsistent or labels are unavailable.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces PMSS, a label-free log parser metric addressing ground-truth limitations, showing strong alignment with existing measures and practical adoption guidance.
AI.motivation: Existing parser evaluation metrics depend on inconsistent, labeled ground-truth data, limiting industrial applicability and causing performance evaluation discrepancies.
AI.method: Proposes PMSS, a label-free metric using medoid silhouette analysis and Lev́enshtein distance for template-level evaluation, validated via comparisons with FGA/FTA on six parsers.
AI.result: PMSS shows 0.648/0.587 Spearman correlations with FGA/FTA (p<1e-8), and top parsers under PMSS/FGA differ by only 2.1%. Discusses metric interpretation challenges and adoption guidelines.
AI.conclusion: PMSS offers a reliable alternative for evaluating log parsers when ground-truths are inconsistent or unavailable, demonstrating strong correlation with existing metrics while addressing their limitations.

title: Enhancing Distributed Authorization With Lagrange Interpolation And Attribute-Based Encryption
comment: In terms of content, the paper meets the requirements of the journal. This paper provides an opportunity for the author to conduct a more detailed experiment and present the results. By doing so, the author's idea and algorithm can be improved. The experiment can be conducted under a variety of conditions
summary: In todays security landscape, every user wants to access large amounts of data with confidentiality and authorization. To maintain confidentiality, various researchers have proposed several techniques. However, to access secure data, researchers use access control lists to grant authentication and provide authorization. The above several steps will increase the server's computation overhead and response time. To cope with these two problems, we proposed multiparty execution on the server. In this paper, we introduce two different approaches. The first approach is encryption, utilizing the Involution Function Based Stream Cipher to encrypt the file data. The second approach is key distribution, using the Shamir secret sharing scheme to divide and distribute the symmetric key to every user. The decryption process required key reconstruction, which used second order Lagrange interpolation to reconstruct the secret keys from the hidden points. The process will reduce the server's computational overhead. The results are evaluated based on the encryption and decryption time, throughput, computational overhead, and security analysis. In the future, the proposed mechanism will be used to share large-scale, secure data within the organization.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using an involution function-based stream cipher for encryption and Shamir secret sharing for key distribution to reduce server overhead.
AI.motivation: Servers experience high computational overhead and slow response times when managing secure data access with traditional methods.
AI.method: First approach: encryption using an Involution Function Based Stream Cipher. Second approach: key distribution using Shamir secret sharing, with key reconstruction via second-order Lagrange interpolation.
AI.result: The proposed method reduces server computational overhead and improves performance metrics such as encryption/decryption time, throughput, and security.
AI.conclusion: The technique offers an efficient solution for secure large-scale data sharing and has potential applications in organizational data management.

title: Analyzing Code Injection Attacks on LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems in Software Development
summary: Agentic AI and Multi-Agent Systems are poised to dominate industry and society imminently. Powered by goal-driven autonomy, they represent a powerful form of generative AI, marking a transition from reactive content generation into proactive multitasking capabilities. As an exemplar, we propose an architecture of a multi-agent system for the implementation phase of the software engineering process. We also present a comprehensive threat model for the proposed system. We demonstrate that while such systems can generate code quite accurately, they are vulnerable to attacks, including code injection. Due to their autonomous design and lack of humans in the loop, these systems cannot identify and respond to attacks by themselves. This paper analyzes the vulnerability of multi-agent systems and concludes that the coder-reviewer-tester architecture is more resilient than both the coder and coder-tester architectures, but is less efficient at writing code. We find that by adding a security analysis agent, we mitigate the loss in efficiency while achieving even better resiliency. We conclude by demonstrating that the security analysis agent is vulnerable to advanced code injection attacks, showing that embedding poisonous few-shot examples in the injected code can increase the attack success rate from 0% to 71.95%.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a multi-agent system for software engineering, analyzes its vulnerabilities, and suggests a coder-reviewer-tester architecture with a security analysis agent to improve resilience while mitigating efficiency loss. However, the security analysis agent itself is susceptible to advanced code injection attacks through poisoned examples.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the security challenges in agentic AI and Multi-Agent Systems (MAS), which are becoming increasingly prevalent in proactive multitasking applications. It highlights the need for resilient architectures as these systems transition from reactive to autonomous content generation in fields like software engineering.
AI.method: The paper proposes an architecture (coder-reviewer-tester) with specific roles for agents in the software implementation phase and evaluates it against threats like code injection. It introduces a security analysis agent to monitor agent-generated code for vulnerabilities and conducts experiments to assess the robustness of this addition against advanced attacks.
AI.result: Experiments showed that the coder-reviewer-tester system is more resilient than the coder and coder-tester systems, reducing the loss in efficiency over the latter two. However, the security analysis agent was found vulnerable to advanced code injection where the inclusion of poisoned few-shot examples increased attack success rates significantly (up to 71.95%).
AI.conclusion: The introduction of a security analysis agent enhances the system's resilience without sacrificing efficiency, but it remains vulnerable to sophisticated attacks involving data poisoning. This underscores the need for improved robustness in multi-agent systems, particularly in secure software development applications.

title: Security Boundaries of Quantum Key Reuse: A Quantitative Evaluation Method for QKD Key Rotation Interval and Security Benefits Combined with Block Ciphers
summary: With the rapid development of quantum computing, classical cryptography systems are facing increasing security threats, making it urgent to build architectures resilient to quantum attacks. Although Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) technology provides information-theoretic security, its limited bandwidth requires it to be combined with classical cryptography-particularly block ciphers such as AES and SM4-in practical deployments.However, when a single key is used to process multiple multi-block files, the resulting reduction in security strength has not yet been systematically quantified.In this work, we focus on the use of both QKD keys and block ciphers, and construct a precise calculation model for the key rotation interval. We further propose a quantitative method to evaluate the security benefit of using QKD keys for block cipher. Building on concrete security models and the security properties of various block cipher modes (CTR, CBC, and ECBC-MAC), we derive the maximum number of files that can be safely encrypted under a single key, denoted Q*, and quantify the benefits of key rotation interval in enhancing security levels. Using SM4 as a case study, our results show that, under an 80-bit security target, uniformly performing k key rotations can increase the security strength by log2(k) to 2log2(k) bits. This study provides theoretical support and a basis for parameter optimization for the integrated application of QKD keys with classical cryptographic algorithms and the engineering deployment of cryptographic systems.

AI.tldr: This paper quantifies the security benefits of QKD-based key rotation intervals in block ciphers, offering a theoretical framework to optimize QKD-classical crypto integration for quantum-resistant systems.
AI.motivation: The rapid development of quantum computing threatens classical cryptography, necessitating architectures resilient to quantum attacks. QKD's limited bandwidth requires combination with classical block ciphers, but the security reduction when using single keys for multiple files remains unquantified.
AI.method: Constructs a precise calculation model for key rotation intervals and proposes a quantitative method to evaluate the security benefits of QKD keys in block ciphers. Derives the maximum number of files (Q*) using concrete security models and analyzes CTR, CBC, and ECBC-MAC modes.
AI.result: Using SM4, the study shows that under an 80-bit security target, uniform key rotations increase security strength by log2(k) to 2log2(k) bits, providing explicit quantitative metrics for optimization.
AI.conclusion: The study provides theoretical support and a basis for parameter optimization in integrating QKD keys with classical cryptographic algorithms, enhancing the security of cryptographic systems against quantum threats.

title: HALF: Process Hollowing Analysis Framework for Binary Programs with the Assistance of Kernel Modules
summary: Binary program analysis is still very important in system security. There are many practical achievements in binary code analysis, but fine-grained analysis such as dynamic taint analysis, is constantly studied due to the problem of deployability, high memory usage, and performance overhead, so as to better adapt to the new analysis scenario, such as memory corruption exploits and sandbox evasion malware. This paper presents a new binary program analysis framework, in order to improve the usability and performance of fine-grained analysis. The framework mainly uses the kernel module to further expand the analysis capability of the traditional dynamic binary instrumentation. Then, based on the idea of decoupling analysis, the analysis environment is constructed in the container process through process hollowing techniques in a new way. It can reuse the functions of the existing dynamic binary instrumentation platforms and also reduce the impact on the execution of the target program. The prototype is implemented on the Windows platform. The validity and performance of the framework are verified by a large number of experiments with benchmark and actual programs. The effectiveness of the framework is also verified by the analysis of actual exploit programs and malicious code, demonstrating the value of the practical application.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a binary analysis framework using kernel modules and containerization to enhance fine-grained analysis performance, validated through experiments showing improved usability and practical application in analyzing security threats.
AI.motivation: Existing fine-grained analysis methods face challenges in deployability, memory consumption, and performance - necessitating adaptation to modern analysis scenarios like memory corruption exploits and sandbox evasion techniques.
AI.method: The framework combines kernel module expansion with decoupled analysis, using process hollowing in containerized environments to reduce overhead while reusing existing dynamic instrumentation platforms.
AI.result: Windows-based prototype validated through benchmark programs, real-world applications, and successful analysis of exploit programs and malware demonstrating effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework improves usability and performance of fine-grained binary analysis, demonstrating practical value in analyzing exploit programs and malware through experiments.

title: Verifiable Passkey: The Decentralized Authentication Standard
summary: Passwordless authentication has revolutionized the way we authenticate across various websites and services. FIDO2 Passkeys, is one of the most-widely adopted standards of passwordless authentication that promises phishing-resistance. However, like any other authentication system, passkeys require the user details to be saved on a centralized server, also known as Relying Party (RP) Server. This has led users to create a new passkey for every new online account. While this just works for a limited number of online accounts, the limited storage space of secure storage modules like TPM or a physical security key limits the number of passkeys a user can have. For example, Yubico Yubikey 5 (firmware 5.0 - 5.6) offers to store only 25 passkeys, while firmware 5.7+ allows to store upto 100 [1]. To overcome this problem, one of the widely adopted approaches is to use Federated Authentication with Single Sign On (SSO). This allows the user to create a passkey for the Identity Provider (IdP) and use the IdP to authenticate to all service providers. This proves to be a significant privacy risk since the IdP can potentially track users across different services. To overcome these limitations, this paper introduces a novel standard 'Verifiable Passkey' that allows the user to use Passkeys created for a Verifiable Credential issuer across any platform without risking privacy or user tracking.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces 'Verifiable Passkey', a new standard that addresses storage and privacy issues with current passwordless authentication methods by allowing reuse of passkeys across platforms without tracking.
AI.motivation: FIDO2 Passkeys face storage limitations due to device constraints and create privacy concerns when using SSO, as the Identity Provider can track users across services.
AI.method: The paper proposes 'Verifiable Passkey', which utilizes Verifiable Credentials to enable cross-platform use of passkeys without compromising privacy or enabling user tracking.
AI.result: The proposed Verifiable Passkey standard offers a solution that extends the usability of passkeys beyond individual services or devices without sacrificing security or privacy.
